industries.name: Aluminum-Scandium Alloys
uses: Scandium addition of 0.1–0.5% to aluminum alloys dramatically refines grain size and prevents recrystallization during welding — producing weld joints with near-parent-metal strength in 5xxx and 7xxx series alloys

Al-Sc alloys used in MiG and Sukhoi fighter aircraft structures, Russian Ilyushin transport frames, and sports equipment (bicycle frames, baseball bats, lacrosse sticks) where weight savings justify premium cost

SCALMALLOY® (Al-Mg-Sc, developed by Airbus subsidiary APWorks) for selective laser melting (SLM) additive manufacturing of aerospace structural components — highest-strength aluminum alloy printable by 3D printing
Potential high-volume automotive and aerospace adoption is constrained entirely by scandium supply — current world production is only ~20–25 tonnes/year, insufficient for large-scale rollout
Scandium-stabilized zirconia (ScSZ) as electrolyte in solid oxide fuel cells — higher ionic conductivity than yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) enables lower operating temperatures

companies.name: Rusal
companies.use: World's largest aluminum producer and primary scandium supplier. Recovers scandium as byproduct of alumina refining in Ukraine (historically) and Russia. Produces Al-Sc master alloys and Scalmalloy precursor.

companies.name: Scandium International Mining
companies.use: Developing the Nyngan scandium deposit in New South Wales, Australia — one of the largest known primary scandium deposits. Aims to become a non-Chinese, non-Russian scandium supply source for Western aerospace.

companies.name: Clean TeQ (Sunrise Energy Metals)
companies.use: Developing the Sunrise cobalt-nickel-scandium project in New South Wales. Scandium would be recovered as a co-product of nickel and cobalt refining, potentially making Australia a significant scandium supplier.

industries.name: Metal Halide Lighting & Electronics
uses: Scandium iodide (ScI₃) added to mercury-arc lamps produces a high-color-rendering, daylight-balanced light source used in stadium lighting, film and television studio lighting, and high-end retail displays
Scandium oxide (Sc₂O₃) as stabilizer in yttria-stabilized zirconia ceramics for thermal barrier coatings on gas turbine blades
Scandium used in high-intensity discharge (HID) lamps — ScI₃ produces the correct color temperature and color rendering index (CRI >90) for professional applications

Thin-film bulk acoustic resonator (FBAR) filters for 5G smartphones: aluminum nitride doped with ~35% scandium (Al₀.₆₅Sc₀.₃₅N) dramatically increases piezoelectric coefficient, enabling smaller, more efficient RF filters

companies.name: Osram (ams-OSRAM)
companies.use: Major lighting manufacturer using scandium iodide in professional metal halide lamps for broadcast studios, film production, and stadium applications. Also developing ScAlN piezoelectric materials for RF filters.

companies.name: Qorvo
companies.use: Leading RF semiconductor company developing scandium-doped aluminum nitride (ScAlN) bulk acoustic wave (BAW) filters for 5G smartphones — potentially a significant new demand source for scandium.

companies.name: Skyworks Solutions
companies.use: Major mobile RF component supplier investing in ScAlN filter technology for 5G frequency bands. ScAlN filters outperform conventional AlN in bandwidth and efficiency for sub-6GHz 5G applications.

industries.name: Solid Oxide Fuel Cells
uses: Scandium-stabilized zirconia (10Sc1CeSZ) electrolyte in solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs) achieves high ionic conductivity at 700–850°C — lower operating temperature than YSZ extends cell lifetime and reduces balance-of-plant costs
SOFC systems using ScSZ electrolytes developed for stationary distributed power generation, combined heat and power (CHP), and auxiliary power units in trucks and aircraft
Scandium stabilization of zirconia also used in thermal barrier coatings for gas turbine blades — higher temperature stability than yttria-stabilized variants extends engine overhaul intervals

companies.name: Bloom Energy
companies.use: Leading solid oxide fuel cell manufacturer. Its Energy Server uses zirconia-based electrolyte cells for on-site power generation at data centers, hospitals, and commercial facilities.

companies.name: Kyocera
companies.use: Japanese ceramics and electronics company developing residential SOFC systems (Ene-Farm) using scandium-containing zirconia electrolytes for combined heat and power in Japanese households.
